text: The power-duration relationship follows a hyperbolic form that resolves to two parameters: critical power (CP) as the asymptote and W' (work doable above CP), which together predict tolerable duration of exercise above CP.
Critical power represents a fatigue threshold that separates exercise intensity domains where physiological responses can be stabilized (below CP) from those where they cannot (above CP).

Setting exercise intensity relative to critical power provides a more unified profile of systemic and intramuscular responses than calibrating relative to metabolically distant parameters such as lactate threshold or VO2max.
Exercise performed above critical power will continue until W' is expended, VO2max is attained, and intolerance is manifested, with the tolerable duration predictable from the CP and W' parameters.

The critical power concept integrates respiratory, metabolic, and contractile physiological profiles within a coherent framework, enabling identification of whether fatigue at a given intensity is primarily central or peripheral in origin.
The hyperbolic power-duration relationship is highly conserved across species, forms of exercise, and individual muscles or muscle groups, supporting its fundamental physiological basis.
